🧭 What Should I Use?

You don't need everything. Most people use 1–2 features.
This guide helps you find exactly what you need in under 2 minutes.
CopilotForge has 4 layers (skills, agents, memory, cookbook) and 24+ recipes. That sounds like a lot. It isn't — because you won't use most of it. Let's figure out what you actually need. Jump to: I am a... · Flowchart · What's the difference? · What to skip · Still lost? · Quick links

"I am a..."

Find yourself in this table. Follow your path. Ignore everything else. | I am... | Start here | Skip everything else | |---------|-----------|---------------------| | A total beginner who's never used AI coding tools | README Quick Start → then try the hello-world recipe | Agents, memory, advanced recipes | | A developer with a project who wants AI help | npx copilotforge init → answer a few questions → done | The wizard picks everything for you | | A developer who just wants code recipes | cookbook/CHEATSHEET.md | Skills, agents, and memory entirely | | Someone interested in AI research / experiments | auto-research + knowledge-wiki recipes | You don't need the wizard or agents | | Someone facing a complex architecture decision | npx copilotforge init --oracle-prime or select Oracle Prime in Q6 | You just need the reasoning framework — no full scaffold required | | A team lead setting up AI for a team | Full GETTING-STARTED.md walkthrough | You'll use everything — no shortcuts |

Decision Flowchart

What do you want to do? `` What do you want to do? │ ├─ "Just show me code I can copy" │ └─ → cookbook/CHEATSHEET.md (pick a recipe, run it) │ ├─ "Set up AI help for my project" │ └─ → npx copilotforge init (the wizard handles everything) │ ├─ "Run experiments / optimize code automatically" │ └─ → cookbook/auto-research.ts │ ├─ "Build a personal knowledge base" │ └─ → cookbook/knowledge-wiki.ts │ ├─ "I want a quick overview of my project" │ └─ → Run npx copilotforge status for a terminal dashboard. │ It shows your plan progress, active skills and agents, │ memory stats, and git summary — all in one view. No setup needed. │ ├─ "I want AI to build my project from a plan" │ └─ → Enable Task automation in Q6. Edit IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md to describe what you want built, then run The Ralph Loop (task-loop recipe). The AI works through tasks autonomously. │ ├─ "I want to build enterprise agents" │ └─ → Copilot Studio Guide (cookbook/copilot-studio-guide.md) │ ├─ "I want to create Power Apps with code" │ └─ → Code Apps Guide (cookbook/code-apps-guide.md) │ ├─ "I want specialized Copilot agent profiles" │ └─ → Custom Agents Guide (cookbook/copilot-agents-guide.md) │ ├─ "I need help with a complex decision or risk assessment" │ └─ → Oracle Prime (npx copilotforge init --oracle-prime) │ Say "deep analysis" or "oracle prime" in Copilot Chat │ for structured scenario modeling and counterfactual reasoning. │ ├─ "I have a plan and want to execute it" │ └─ → Say "run the plan" to start The Ralph Loop — it reads │ IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md and works through tasks autonomously. │ See also: cookbook/task-loop.ts (code version). │ ├─ "I want to understand how it all fits together" │ └─ → docs/HOW-IT-WORKS.md │ └─ "I don't know what I want" └─ → Start with hello-world.ts — the simplest possible recipe `

What's the Difference?

Skills vs Agents vs Recipes

Think of it like a kitchen:
  • A skill is like a recipe card — written instructions for ONE task (e.g., "how to review code"). It's a markdown file that tells your AI what to do.
  • An agent is like a team member — a chef who knows which recipe cards to grab and when. It has a role, a personality, and a set of skills.
  • A recipe (cookbook) is a finished dish with the full code — copy-paste and run right now. No setup required.
💡 Key insight: You can use recipes without skills or agents. They're completely independent.

auto-research vs The Ralph Loop

auto-research — Your AI tries changes to your code, runs tests, keeps improvements, discards failures. Like a tireless intern running experiments overnight.
  • Best for: optimization, performance tuning, "make this faster."
  • Example: "Try 20 different caching strategies and keep the fastest one."
The Ralph Loop (powered by task-loop.{ext}) — Your AI picks a task from a plan, implements it, validates it, commits, moves to the next. Like a tireless worker who never stops until the plan is complete.
  • Best for: knocking out a backlog of tasks or building a project from a plan.
  • Example: "Here are 15 small bug fixes. Do them all, one by one."

knowledge-wiki vs forge-memory

knowledge-wiki — A personal Wikipedia. You drop in articles, notes, and docs. The AI organizes them into a searchable wiki with cross-references.
  • For: research, learning, business intelligence.
  • You set it up yourself. You feed it content.
forge-memory — CopilotForge's built-in memory. It remembers your project decisions and coding conventions so the wizard gets smarter over time.
  • For: project setup. Created automatically by the wizard.
  • You don't manage it — it manages itself.
💡 These are different things. You don't pick one over the other. knowledge-wiki is a tool you use; forge-memory is a system that runs in the background.

FORGE.md vs README.md vs SKILL.md

| File | What it is | Who reads it | |------|-----------|-------------| | FORGE.md | Your project's control panel (generated by the wizard) | You — to see what's set up | | README.md | The repo's welcome page | Everyone — the front door | | SKILL.md | Instructions for an AI assistant (lives in .github/skills/) | Your AI — tells it how to do a job |

You DON'T Need...

This is your permission slip to skip things. Seriously.
  • ❌ You don't need agents if you just want code recipes → go straight to cookbook/
  • ❌ You don't need memory if this is a one-time project → say "no" to Question 3 in the wizard
  • ❌ You don't need the cookbook if you just want AI-assisted development → the wizard + skills are enough
  • ❌ You don't need to understand skills to use CopilotForge → the wizard creates them for you
  • ❌ You don't need ALL 24 recipes → the wizard picks the right ones for your stack
  • ❌ You don't need Oracle Prime for simple coding tasks → it auto-scales, but is most valuable for complex architecture decisions and risk analysis
  • ❌ You don't need npm or Node.js to use CopilotForge → manual copy works fine (only recipes need a runtime)
  • ❌ You don't need to read every doc → this page points you to the one you need

Still Not Sure?

Three safe starting points — pick any one: 1. Just run the wizard. Type npx copilotforge init and answer the questions. It figures everything out. 2. Try the simplest recipe. Open cookbook/hello-world.ts`. Copy it. Run it. See what happens. 3. Ask your AI assistant. Paste this into your AI chat: "Read FORGE.md and help me get started with CopilotForge." Any of these gets you unstuck in under 5 minutes.

Quick Links

Every doc in the project, with a one-line description. | Doc | What it's for | |-----|--------------| | README.md | The front door — what CopilotForge is and Quick Start | | GETTING-STARTED.md | Full walkthrough from zero to a scaffolded project | | HOW-IT-WORKS.md | Under-the-hood explanation of skills, agents, memory, and the planner | | FAQ.md | Quick answers to common questions | | cookbook/CHEATSHEET.md | Pick a goal → find a recipe → copy it | | cookbook/README.md | How to install and run cookbook recipes | | You are here → WHAT-TO-USE.md | This guide — helps you find the right starting point |
You don't need to read all the docs. You just needed this one. ✨
